html:
XII

The Mother

I

The happiness Mrs.¬†Hilary now enjoyed was of the religious typeвБ†вАФa deep, warm glow, which did not lack excitement. She felt as those may be presumed to feel who have just been converted to some churchвБ†вАФnewly alive, and sunk in spiritual peace, and in profound harmony with life. Where were the old rubs, frets, jars and ennuis? Vanished, melted like yesterdayвАЩs snows in the sun of this new peace. It was as if she had cast her burden upon the Lord. That, said her psychoanalyst doctor, was quite in order; that was what it ought to be like. That was, in effect, what she had in point of fact done; only the place of the Lord was filled by himself. To put the matter briefly, transference of burden had been effected; Mrs.¬†Hilary had laid all her cares, all her perplexities, all her grief, upon this quiet, acute-looking man, who sat with her twice a week for an hour, drawing her out, arranging her symptoms for her, penetrating the hidden places of her soul, looking like a cross between Sherlock Holmes and Mr.¬†Henry Ainley. Her confidence in him was, he told her, the expression of the father-image, which surprised Mrs.¬†Hilary a little, because he was twenty years her junior.

Mrs.¬†Hilary felt that she was getting to know herself very well indeed. Seeing herself through Mr.¬†CradockвАЩs mind, she felt that she was indeed a curious jumble of complexes, of strange, mysterious impulses, desires and fears. Alarming, even horrible in some ways; so that often she thought вАЬCan he be right about me? Am I really like that? Do I really hope that Marjorie (JimвАЩs wife) will die, so that Jim and I may be all in all to each other again? Am I really so wicked?вАЭ But Mr.¬†Cradock said that it was not at all wicked, perfectly natural and normalвБ†вАФthe Unconscious was like that. And worse than that; how much worse he had to break to Mrs.¬†Hilary, who was refined and easily shocked, by gentle hints and slow degrees, lest she should be shocked to death. Her dreams, which she had to recount to him at every sitting, bore such terrible significanceвБ†вАФthey grew worse and worse in proportion, as Mrs.¬†Hilary could stand more.

вАЬAh well,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary sighed uneasily, after an interpretation into strange terms of a dream she had about bathing, вАЬitвАЩs very odd, when IвАЩve never even thought about things like that.вАЭ

вАЬYour Unconscious,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Cradock, firmly, вАЬhas thought the more. The more your Unconscious is obsessed by a thing, the less your conscious self thinks of it. It is shy of the subject, for that very reason.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hilary was certainly shy of the subject, for that reason or others. When she felt too shy of it, Mr.¬†Cradock let her change it. вАЬIt may be true,вАЭ she would say, вАЬbut itвАЩs very terrible, and I would rather not dwell on it.вАЭ

So he would let her dwell instead on the early days of her married life, or on the childrenвАЩs childhood, or on her love for Neville and Jim, or on her impatience with her mother.

II

They were happy little times, stimulating, cosy little times. They spoke straight to the heart, easing it of its weight of tragedy. A splendid man, Mr.¬†Cradock, with his shrewd, penetrating sympathy, his kind firmness. He would listen with interest to everything; the sharp words she had had with Grandmama, troubles with the maids, the little rubs of daily life (and what a rubbing business life is, to be sure!) as well as to profounder, more tragic accounts of desolation, jealousy, weariness and despair. He would say вАЬYour case is a very usual one,вАЭ so that she did not feel ashamed of being like that. He reduced it all, dispassionately and yet not unsympathetically, and with clear scientific precision, to terms of psychical and physical laws. He trained his patient to use her mind and her will, as well as to remember her dreams and to be shocked at nothing that they signified.

Mrs.¬†Hilary would wake each morning, or during the night, and clutch at the dream which was flying from her, clutch and secure it, and make it stand and deliver its outlines to her. She was content with outlines; it was for Mr.¬†Cradock to supply the interpretation. Sometimes, if Mrs.¬†Hilary couldnвАЩt remember any dreams, he would supply, according to a classic precedent, the dream as well as the interpretation. But on the whole, deeply as she revered and admired him, Mrs.¬†Hilary preferred to remember her own dreams; what they meant was bad enough, but the meaning of the dreams that Mr.¬†Cradock told her she had dreamt was beyond all words.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That terrible Unconscious! Mrs.¬†Hilary disliked it excessively; she felt rather as if it were a sewer, sunk beneath an inadequate grating.

But from Mr.¬†Cradock she put up with hearing about it. She would have put up with anything. He was so steadying and so wonderful. He enabled her to face life with a new poise, a fresh lease of strength and vitality. She told Grandmama so. Grandmama said вАЬYes, my dear, IвАЩve observed it in you. It sounds to me an unpleasing business, but it is obviously doing you good, so far. I only wish it may last. The danger may be reaction, after you have finished the course and lost touch with this young man.вАЭ (Mr.¬†Cradock was forty-five, but Grandmama, it must be remembered, was eighty-four.) вАЬYou will have to guard against that. In a way it was a pity you didnвАЩt take up churchgoing instead; religion lasts.вАЭ

вАЬAnd these quackeries do not,вАЭ Grandmama finished her sentence to herself, not wishing to be discouraging.

вАЬNot always,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary truly replied, meaning that religion did not always last.

вАЬNo,вАЭ Grandmama agreed. вАЬUnfortunately not always. Particularly when it is High Church. There was your uncle Bruce, of course.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Mrs.¬†HilaryвАЩs uncle Bruce, who had been High Church for a season, and had even taken Orders in the year 1860, but whose faith had wilted in the heat and toil of the day, so that by 1870 he was an agnostic barrister, took Grandmama back through the last century, and she became reminiscent over the Tractarian movement, and, later, the Ritualists.

вАЬThe Queen never could abide them,вАЭ said Grandmama. вАЬNor could Lord Beaconsfield, nor your father, though he was always kind and tolerant. I remember when Dr.¬†Jowett came to stay with us, how they talked about it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Ah well, theyвАЩve become very prominent since then, and done a great deal of good work, and there are many very able, excellent men and women among them.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But theyвАЩre not High Church any longer, they tell me. TheyвАЩre Catholics in these days. I donвАЩt know enough of them to judge them, but I donвАЩt think they can have the dignity of the old High Church party, for if they had I canвАЩt imagine that GilbertвАЩs wife, for instance, would have joined them, even for so short a time as she did.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, it suits some people, and psychoanalysis obviously suits others. Only I do hope you will try to keep moderate and balanced, my child, and not believe all this young man tells you. Parts of it do sound so very strange.вАЭ

(But Mrs.¬†Hilary would not have dreamt of repeating to Grandmama the strangest parts of all.)

вАЬI feel a new woman,вАЭ she said, fervently, and Grandmama smiled, well pleased, thinking that it certainly did seem rather like the old evangelical conversions of her youth. (Which, of course, did not always last, any more than the High Church equivalents did.)

All Grandmama committed herself to, in her elderly caution, which came however less from age than from having known Mrs.¬†Hilary for sixty-three years, was вАЬWell, well, we must see.вАЭ

III

And then RosalindвАЩs letter came. It came by the afternoon postвБ†вАФthe big, mauve, scented, sprawled sheets, dashingly monographed across one corner.

вАЬGilbertвАЩs wife,вАЭ pronounced Grandmama, non-committally from her easy chair, and, said in that tone, it was quite sufficient comment. вАЬAnother cup of tea, please, Emily.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hilary gave it to her, then began to read aloud the letter from GilbertвАЩs wife. GilbertвАЩs wife was one of the topics upon which she and Grandmama were in perfect accord, only that Mrs.¬†Hilary was irritated when Grandmama pushed the responsibility for the relationship onto her by calling Rosalind вАЬyour daughter-in-law.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hilary began to read the letter in the tone used by well-bred women when they would, if in a slightly lower social stratum, say вАЬFancy that now! Did you ever, the brazen hussy!вАЭ Grandmama listened, cynically disapproving, prepared to be disgusted yet entertained. On the whole she thoroughly enjoyed letters from GilbertвАЩs wife. She settled down comfortably in her chair with her second cup of tea, while Mrs.¬†Hilary read two pages of what Grandmama called вАЬfoolish chitchat.вАЭ RosalindвАЩs letters were really like the gossipping imbecilities written by Eve of the Tatler, or the other ladies who enliven our shinier-paper weeklies with their bright personal babble. She did not often waste one of them on her mother-in-law; only when she had something to say which might annoy her.

вАЬDo you hear from Nan?вАЭ the third page of the letter began. вАЬI hear from the Bramertons, who are wintering in RomeвБ†вАФthe Charlie Bramertons, you know, great friends of mine and GilbertвАЩs (he won a pot of money on the Derby this year and theyвАЩve a dinky flat in some palace out thereвБ†вАФ), and they meet Nan about, and sheвАЩs always with Stephen Lumley, the painter (rotten painter, if you ask me, but heвАЩs somehow diddled London into admiring him, donвАЩt expect youвАЩve heard of him down at the seaside). Well, theyвАЩre quite simply always together, and the Brams say that everyone out there says it isnвАЩt in the least an ambiguous caseвБ†вАФno two ways about it. He doesnвАЩt live with his wife, you know. YouвАЩll excuse me passing this on to you, but it does seem you ought to know. I mentioned it to Neville the other day, just before the poor old dear went down with the plague, but you know what Neville is, she always sticks up for Nan and doesnвАЩt care what she does, or what people say. People are talking; beasts, arenвАЩt they! But thatвАЩs the way of this wicked old world, we all do it. GilbertвАЩs quite upset about it, says Nan ought to manage her affairs more quietly. But after all and between you and me itвАЩs not the first time NanвАЩs been a Town Topic, is it.

вАЬHowвАЩs the psycho going? IsnвАЩt Cradock rather a priceless pearl? YouвАЩre over head and ears with him by now, of course, we all are. Psycho wouldnвАЩt do you any good if you werenвАЩt, thatвАЩs the truth. Cradock told me himself once that transference canвАЩt be effected without the patient being a little bit smitten. Personally I should give up a man patient at once if he didnвАЩt rather like me. But isnвАЩt it soothing and comforting, and doesnвАЩt it make you feel good all over, like a hot bath when youвАЩre fagged out.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

But Mrs.¬†Hilary didnвАЩt get as far as this. She stopped at вАЬnot the first time NanвАЩs been a Town Topic.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ and dropped the thin mauve sheets onto her lap, and looked at Grandmama, her face queerly tight and flushed, as if she were about to cry.

Grandmama had finished her tea, and had been listening quietly.

Mrs.¬†Hilary said вАЬOh, my God,вАЭ and jerked her head back, quivering like a nervous horse who has had a shock and does not care to conceal it.

вАЬYour daughter-in-law,вАЭ said Grandmama, without excitement, вАЬis an exceedingly vulgar young woman.вАЭ

вАЬVulgar? Rosalind? But of course.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Only that doesnвАЩt affect Nan.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYour daughter-in-law,вАЭ Grandmama added, вАЬis also a very notorious liar.вАЭ

вАЬA liarвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ oh yes, yes, yes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But this time itвАЩs true. Oh I feel, I know, itвАЩs true. Nan would. That Stephen LumleyвБ†вАФheвАЩs been hanging about her for ages.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh yes, itвАЩs true what they say. The very worst.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Grandmama glanced at her curiously. The very worst in that direction had become strangely easier of credence by Mrs.¬†Hilary lately. Grandmama had observed that. Mr.¬†CradockвАЩs teaching had not been without its effect. According to Mr.¬†Cradock, people were usually engaged either in practising the very worst, or in desiring to practise it, or in wishing and dreaming that they had practised it. It was the nature of mankind, and not in the least reprehensible, though curable. Thus Mr.¬†Cradock. Mrs.¬†Hilary had, against her own taste, absorbed part of his teaching, but nothing could ever persuade her that it was not reprehensible: it quite obviously was. Also disgusting. Mr.¬†Cradock might say what he liked. It was disgusting. And when the man had a wife.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬIt is awful,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hilary. вАЬAwful.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It must be stopped. I shall go to Rome. At once.вАЭ

вАЬThat wonвАЩt stop it, dear, if it is going on. It will only irritate the young people.вАЭ

вАЬIrritate! You can use a word like that! Mother, you donвАЩt realise this ghastly thing.вАЭ

вАЬI quite see, my dear, that Nan may be carrying on with this artist. And very wrong it is, if so. All I say is that your going to Rome wonвАЩt stop it. You know that you and Nan donвАЩt always get on very smoothly. You rub each other up.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It would be far better if someone else went. Neville, say.вАЭ

вАЬNeville is ill.вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary shut her lips tightly on that. She was glad Neville was ill; she had always hated (she could not help it) the devotion between Neville and Nan. Nan, in her tempestuous childhood, flaring with rage against her mother, or sullen, spiteful and perverse, long before she could have put into words the qualities in Mrs.¬†Hilary which made her like that, had always gone to Neville, nine years older, to be soothed and restored to good temper. Neville had reprimanded the little naughty sister, had told her she must be вАЬdecent to motherвБ†вАФfeel decent if you can, behave decent in any case,вАЭ was the way she had put it. It was Neville who had heard NanвАЩs confidences and helped her out of scrapes in childhood, schoolgirlhood and ever since. This was very bitter to Mrs.¬†Hilary. She was jealous of both of them; jealous that so much of NevilleвАЩs love should go elsewhere than to her, jealous that Nan, who gave her nothing except generous and extravagant gifts and occasional, spasmodic, remorseful efforts at affection and gentleness, should to Neville give all.

вАЬNeville is ill,вАЭ she said. вАЬShe certainly wonвАЩt be fit to travel out of England this winter. Influenza coming on the top of that miserable breakdown is a thing to be treated with the greatest care. Even when she is recovered, post-influenza will keep her weak till the summer. I am really anxious about her. No; Neville is quite out of the question.вАЭ

вАЬWell, what about Pamela?вАЭ

вАЬPamela is up to her eyes in her work.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Besides, why should Pamela go, or Neville, rather than I? A girlвАЩs mother is obviously the right person. I may not be of much use to my children in these days, but at least I hope I can save them from themselves.вАЭ

вАЬIt takes a clever parent to do that, Emily,вАЭ said Grandmama, who doubtless knew.

вАЬBut, mother, what would you have me do? Sit with my hands before me while my daughter lives in sin? WhatвАЩs your plan?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm too old to make plans, dear. I can only look on at the world. IвАЩve looked at the world now for many, many years, and IвАЩve learnt that only great wisdom and great love can change peopleвАЩs decisions as to their way of life, or turn them from evil courses. Frankly, my child, I doubt if you have, where Nan is concerned, enough wisdom or enough love. Enough sympathy, I should rather say, for you have love. But do you feel you understand the child enough to interfere wisely and successfully?вАЭ

вАЬOh, you think IвАЩm a fool, mother; of course I know youвАЩve always thought me a fool. Good God, if a mother canвАЩt interfere with her own daughter to save her from wickedness and disaster, who can, I should like to know?вАЭ

вАЬOne would indeed like to know that,вАЭ Grandmama said, sadly.

вАЬPerhaps youвАЩd like to go yourself,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary shot at her, quivering now with anger and feeling.

вАЬNo, my dear. Even if I were able to get to Rome I should know that I was too old to interfere with the lives of the young. I donвАЩt understand them enough. You believe that you do. Well, I suppose you must go and try. I canвАЩt stop you.вАЭ

вАЬYou certainly canвАЩt. Nothing can stop me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ YouвАЩre singularly unsympathetic, mother, about this awful business.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt feel so, dear. I am very, very sorry for you, and very, very sorry for Nan (whom, you must remember, we may be slandering). I have always looked on unlawful love as a very great sin, though there may be great provocation to it.вАЭ

вАЬIt is an awful sin.вАЭ Mr.¬†Cradock could say what he liked on that subject; he might tell Mrs.¬†Hilary that it was not awful except in so far as any other yielding to natureвАЩs promptings in defiance of the law of man was awful, but he could not persuade her. Like many other people, she set that particular sin apart, in a special place by itself; she would talk of вАЬa bad woman,вАЭ вАЬan immoral man,вАЭ a girl who had вАЬlost her character,вАЭ and mean merely the one kind of badness, the one manifestation of immorality, the one element in character. Dishonesty and cruelty she could forgive, but never that.

вАЬI shall start in three days,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hilary, becoming tragically resolute. вАЬI must tell Mr.¬†Cradock tomorrow.вАЭ

вАЬThat young man? Must he know about NanвАЩs affairs, my dear?вАЭ

вАЬI have to tell him everything, mother. ItвАЩs part of the course. He is as secret as the grave.вАЭ

Grandmama knew that Emily, less secret than the grave, would have to ease herself of the sad tale to someone or other in the course of the next day, and supposed that it had better be to Mr.¬†Cradock, who seemed to be a kind of hybrid of doctor and clergyman, and so presumably was more discreet than an ordinary human being. Emily must tell. Emily always would. That was why she enjoyed this foolish psychoanalysis business so much.

At the very thought of it a gleam had brightened Mrs.¬†HilaryвАЩs eyes, and her rigid, tense pose had relaxed. Oh the comfort of telling Mr.¬†Cradock! Even if he did tell her how it was all in the course of nature, at least he would sympathise with her trouble about it, and her annoyance with Grandmama. And he would tell her how best to deal with Nan when she got to her. NanвАЩs was the sort of case that Mr.¬†Cradock really did understand. Any situation between the sexesвБ†вАФhe was all over it. Psychoanalysts adored sex; they made an idol of it. They communed with it, as devotees with their God. They couldnвАЩt really enjoy, with their whole minds, anything else, Mrs.¬†Hilary sometimes vaguely felt. But as, like the gods of the other devotees, it was to them immanent, everywhere and in everything; they could be always happy. If they went up into heaven it was there; if they fled down into hell it was there also. Once, when Mrs.¬†Hilary had tentatively suggested that Freud, for instance, overstated its importance, Mr.¬†Cradock had said firmly вАЬIt is impossible to do that,вАЭ which settled it once and for all.

Mrs.¬†Hilary stood up. Her exalted, tragic mood clothed her like a flowing garment.

вАЬI shall write to Cook,вАЭ she said. вАЬAlso to Nan, to tell her I am coming.вАЭ

Grandmama, after a momentвАЩs silence, seemed to gather herself together for a final effort.

вАЬEmily, my child. Is your mind set to do this?вАЭ

вАЬAbsolutely, mother. Absolutely and entirely.вАЭ

вАЬShall I tell you what I think? No, you donвАЩt want to hear it, but you drive me to it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If you go to that foolish, reckless child and attempt to interfere with her, or even to question her, you will run the risk, if she is innocent, of driving her into what you are trying to prevent. If she is already committed to it, you run the risk of shutting the door against her return. In either case you will alienate her from yourself: that is the least of the risks you run, though the most certain.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That is all. I can say no more. But I ask you, my dear.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I beg you, for the childвАЩs sake and your ownвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ to write neither to Cook nor to Nan.вАЭ

GrandmamaвАЩs breath came rather fast and heavily; her heart was troubling her; emotion and effort were not good for it.

Mrs.¬†Hilary stood looking down at the old shrunk figure, shaking a little as she stood, knowing that she must be patient and calm.

вАЬYou will please allow me to judge. You will please let me take the steps I think necessary to help my child. I know that you have no confidence in my judgment or my tact; youвАЩve always shown that plainly enough, and done your best to teach my children the same view of me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Grandmama put up her hand, meaning that she could not stand, neither she nor her heart could stand, a scene. Mrs.¬†Hilary broke off. For once she did not want a scene either. In these days she found what vent was necessary for her emotional system in her interviews with Mr.¬†Cradock.

вАЬI daresay you mean well, mother. But in this matter I must be the judge. I am a mother first and foremost. It is the only thing that life has left for me to be.вАЭ (Scarcely a daughter, she meant: that was made too difficult for her; you would almost imagine that the office was not wanted.)

She turned to the writing table.

вАЬFirst of all I shall write to Rosalind, and tell her what I think of her and her abominable gossip.вАЭ

She began to write.

Grandmama sat shrunk and old and tired in her chair.

Mrs.¬†HilaryвАЩs pen scratched over the paper, telling Rosalind what she thought.

вАЬDear Rosalind,вАЭ she wrote, вАЬI was very much surprised at your letter. I do not know why you should trouble to repeat to me these ridiculous stories about Nan. You cannot suppose that I am likely to care either what you or any of your friends are saying about one of my children.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ And so on. One knows the style. It eases the mind of the writer and does not deceive the reader. When the reader is Rosalind Hilary it amuses her vastly.

IV

Next day, at three p.m., Mrs.¬†Hilary told Mr.¬†Cradock all about it. Mr.¬†Cradock was not in the least surprised. Nor had he the slightest, not the remotest doubt that Nan and Stephen Lumley were doing what Mrs.¬†Hilary called living in sin, what he preferred to call obeying the natural ego. (After all, as any theologian would point out, the terms are synonymous in a fallen world.)

вАЬI must have your advice,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary said. вАЬYou must tell me what line to take with her.вАЭ

вАЬShall you,вАЭ Mr.¬†Cradock enquired, thoughtful and intelligent, вАЬfind your daughter in a state of conflict?вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hilary spread her hands helplessly before her.

вАЬI know nothing; nothing.вАЭ

вАЬA very great deal,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Cradock, вАЬdepends on that. If she is torn between the cravings of the primitive ego and the inhibitions put upon these cravings by the conventions of societyвБ†вАФif, in fact, her censor, her endopsychic censor, is still functioning.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬOh, I doubt if NanвАЩs got an endopsychic censor. She is so lawless always.вАЭ

вАЬEvery psyche has a censor.вАЭ Mr.¬†Cradock was firm. вАЬRegarded, of course, by the psyche with very varying degrees of respect. Well, what I mean to say is, if your daughter is in a state of conflict, with forces pulling her both ways, her case will be very much easier to deal with than if she has let her primitive ego so take possession of the situation that she feels in a state of harmony. In the former case, you will only have to strengthen the forces which are opposing her sexual craving.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hilary fidgeted uneasily. вАЬOh, I donвАЩt think Nan feels that exactly. None of my children.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Mr.¬†Cradock gave her an amused glance. It seemed sometimes that he would never get this foolish lady properly educated.

вАЬYour children, I presume, are human, Mrs.¬†Hilary. Sexual craving means a craving for intimacy with a member of another sex.вАЭ

вАЬOh well, I suppose it does. I donвАЩt care for the name, somehow. But please go on.вАЭ

вАЬI was going to say, if you find, on the other hand, that your daughterвАЩs nature has attained harmony in connection with this course she is pursuing, your task will be far more difficult. You will then have to create a discord, instead of merely strengthening it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ May I ask your daughterвАЩs age?вАЭ

вАЬNan is thirty-three.вАЭ

вАЬA dangerous age.вАЭ

вАЬAll NanвАЩs ages,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hilary, вАЬhave been dangerous. Nan is like that.вАЭ

вАЬAs to that,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Cradock, вАЬwe may say that all ages are dangerous to all people, in this dangerous life we live. But the thirties are a specially dangerous time for women. They have outlived the shynesses and restraints of girlhood, and not attained to the caution and discretion of middle age. They are reckless, and consciously or unconsciously on the lookout for adventure. They see ahead of them the end of youth, and that quickens their pace.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Has passion always been a strong element in your daughterвАЩs life?вАЭ

вАЬOh, passion.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ (Another word not liked by Mrs.¬†Hilary.) вАЬNot quite that, I should say. Nan has been reckless; she has got into scrapes, got herself talked about. She has played about with men a good deal always. But as to passion.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬA common thing enough,вАЭ Mr.¬†Cradock told her, as it were reassuringly. вАЬNothing to fight shy of, or be afraid of. But something to be regulated of course.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Now, the thing is to oppose to this irregular desire of your daughterвАЩs for this man a new and a stronger set of desires. Fight one group of complexes with another. You canвАЩt, I suppose, persuade her to be analysed? There are good analysts in Rome.вАЭ

вАЬOh no. Nan laughs at it. She laughs at everything of that sort.вАЭ

вАЬA great mistake. A mistake often made by shallow and foolish people. They might as well laugh at surgery.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well now, to go into this question of the battle between the complex-groups.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

He went into it, patiently and exhaustively. His phrases drifted over Mrs.¬†HilaryвАЩs head.

вАЬвА¶¬†a deterrent force residing in the ego and preventing us from stepping outside the bounds of propriety.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Rebellious messages sent up from the Unconscious, which wishes to live, love and act in archaic modesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ conflict with the progress of human societyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ inhibitory and repressive power of the censor.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ (How wonderful, thought Mrs.¬†Hilary, to be able to talk so like a book for so long together!)вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬgive the censor all the help we canвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ keep the Unconscious in order by turning its energies into some other channelвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ give it a substitute.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The energy involved in the intense desire for someone of another sex can be divertedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ employed on some useful work. LibidoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ it should all be used. Find another channel for your daughterвАЩs libido.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Her life is perhaps a rather vacant one?вАЭ

That Mrs.¬†Hilary was able to reply to.

вАЬNanвАЩs? Vacant? Oh no. She is quite full of energy. Too full. Always doing a thousand things. And she writes, you know.вАЭ

вАЬAh. That should be an outlet. A great deal of libido is used up by that. Well, her present strong desire for this man should be sublimated into a desire for something else. I gather that her root trouble is lawlessness. That can be cured. You must make her remember her first lawless action.вАЭ (ManвАЩs first disobedience and the fruit thereof, thought Mrs.¬†Hilary.)

вАЬO dear me,вАЭ she said, вАЬIвАЩm afraid that would be impossible. When she was a month old she used to attempt to dash her bottle onto the floor.вАЭ

вАЬPeople have even remembered their baptisms, when driven back to them by analysis.вАЭ

вАЬOur children were not baptised. My husband was something of a Unitarian. He said he would not tie them up with a rite against which they might react in later life. So they were merely registered.вАЭ

вАЬAh. In a way that is a pity. Baptism is an impressive moment in the sensitive consciousness of the infant. It has sometimes been found to be a sort of lamp shining through the haze of the early memory. Registration, owing to the nonparticipation of the infant, is useless in that way.вАЭ

вАЬNan might remember how she kicked me when I short-coated her,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary mused, hopefully.

Mr.¬†Cradock flowed on. Mrs.¬†Hilary, listened, assented, was impressed. It all sounded so simple, so wonderful, even so beautiful. But she thought once or twice, вАЬHe doesnвАЩt know Nan.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ she said, rising to go when her hour was over. вАЬYou have made me feel so much stronger, as usual. I canвАЩt thank you enough for all you do for me. I could face none of my troubles and problems but for your help.вАЭ

вАЬThat merely means,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Cradock, who always got the last word, вАЬthat your ego is at present in what is called the state of infantile dependence or tutelage. A necessary but an impermanent stage in its struggle towards the adult level of the reality-principle.вАЭ

вАЬI suppose so,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hilary said. вАЬGoodbye.вАЭ

вАЬHe is too clever for me,вАЭ she thought, as she went home. вАЬHe is often above my head.вАЭ But she was used to that in the people she met.
